title_en: The Preservation of the Quran: A Case Study in Textual Integrity
title_ar: The Preservation of the Quran: A Case Study in Textual Integrity
title_id: Pemeliharaan Al-Qur'an: Studi Kasus Integritas Tekstual
content_id:
Di antara bagian-bagian Izhar al-Haqq yang paling menarik adalah perbandingan panjang al-Kairanawi antara transmisi Al-Qur'an dan transmisi teks-teks Alkitab. Perbandingan ini mengacu pada kontras yang sama yang telah digambarkan Ibnu Taimiyah dalam al-Jawab al-Sahih tetapi mengembangkannya dengan detail yang jauh lebih besar dan dengan rujukan eksplisit pada bukti yang telah dikumpulkan kajian Alkitab Eropa abad kesembilan belas tentang tradisi manuskrip Alkitab. Wawasan al-Kairanawi adalah bahwa alat-alat kritik tekstual yang sama yang digunakan para misionaris untuk mengklaim superioritas wahyu Kristen atas tradisi-tradisi sebelumnya dapat dibalikkan untuk menunjukkan keandalan tekstual Al-Qur'an yang lebih unggul dibandingkan Alkitab.

Pemeliharaan Al-Qur'an bertumpu pada fondasi ganda yang unik di antara kitab-kitab suci utama dunia: transmisi tertulis dan lisan secara bersamaan yang saling memeriksa satu sama lain. Sejak tahun-tahun paling awal pewahyuan, Nabi memerintahkan agar setiap wahyu baru ditulis oleh juru tulis yang ditunjuk dan juga dihafal oleh para Sahabat. Komunitas yang terbentuk di sekitar Nabi mencakup, sejak awal, sejumlah besar huffaz (mereka yang menghafal seluruh Al-Qur'an), dan tradisi hafalan lengkap telah berlanjut tanpa terputus dari hari itu hingga sekarang. Seorang anak Muslim yang menghafal Al-Qur'an hari ini tidak sekadar belajar dari buku; mereka menerima tradisi lisan yang terhubung dalam rantai transmisi guru-ke-murid yang tidak terputus kembali kepada Nabi sendiri. Rantai transmisi untuk qira'at (mode bacaan) yang kanonik sepenuhnya terdokumentasi dalam ilmu-ilmu tradisional bacaan.

Al-Kairanawi membandingkan ini dengan tradisi manuskrip Perjanjian Baru, yang manuskrip lengkap tertua bertanggal dari abad keempat, lebih dari tiga ratus tahun setelah peristiwa-peristiwa yang mereka gambarkan, dan yang para sarjana telah mengidentifikasi ribuan varian di seluruh tradisi manuskrip yang bertahan. Ia mengutip temuan ilmiah bahwa tidak ada dua manuskrip Perjanjian Baru yang sepenuhnya identik, mencerminkan variasi-variasi yang tak terelakkan yang muncul dalam penyalinan tulisan tangan selama banyak generasi. Ia mencatat perdebatan tekstual yang signifikan tentang bagian-bagian tertentu: akhir dari Markus, kisah wanita yang tertangkap berzina dalam Yohanes, koma Johanneum dalam surat pertama Yohanes yang secara eksplisit mendukung Trinitas dan muncul dalam Vulgata Latin tetapi tidak dalam manuskrip-manuskrip Yunani tertua. Ini bukan masalah-masalah pinggiran; mereka melibatkan bagian-bagian yang secara doktrinal penting dalam teologi Kristen.

Signifikansi perbandingan ini bagi argumen keseluruhan al-Kairanawi adalah substansial. Pfander dan para misionaris lainnya telah mengklaim bahwa teks kitab suci Kristen yang jelas dan andal memberikan dasar yang lebih unggul untuk kepastian keagamaan dibandingkan sumber-sumber Islam. Al-Kairanawi menunjukkan bahwa kepercayaan diri dalam keandalan tekstual Alkitab ini tidak dijamin oleh bukti aktual dari tradisi manuskrip. Jika keandalan tekstual adalah ukuran otoritas keagamaan, maka Al-Qur'an, dengan tradisi transmisi lisannya yang luar biasa dan ketiadaan lengkap dari variasi manuskrip yang menjadi ciri khas teks-teks Alkitab, berdiri di atas tanah yang jauh lebih tinggi daripada Alkitab. Argumen ini secara efektif menetralisir salah satu keunggulan retoris utama yang diklaim para misionaris Kristen, dan ia melakukannya dengan melibatkan tradisi Kristen berdasarkan ketentuan-ketentuannya sendiri, menggunakan bukti yang dikumpulkan oleh para sarjana Kristen itu sendiri.
content_en:
Among the most compelling sections of Izhar al-Haqq is al-Kairanawi's extended comparison between the transmission of the Quran and the transmission of the Biblical texts. This comparison draws on the same contrast that Ibn Taymiyyah had sketched in al-Jawab al-Sahih but develops it with much greater detail and with explicit reference to the evidence that nineteenth-century European Biblical scholarship had assembled about the manuscript tradition of the Bible. Al-Kairanawi's insight was that the same tools of textual criticism that missionaries used to claim the superiority of Christian revelation over earlier traditions could be turned around to demonstrate the Quran's superior textual reliability compared to the Bible.

The Quran's preservation rests on a dual foundation that is unique among the world's major scriptures: simultaneous written and oral transmission, mutually checking each other. From the earliest years of the revelation, the Prophet commanded that each new revelation be written down by designated scribes and also committed to memory by the Companions. The community that formed around the Prophet included, from the outset, large numbers of huffaz (those who memorized the entire Quran), and the tradition of complete memorization has continued unbroken from that day to the present. A Muslim child memorizing the Quran today is not merely learning from a book; they are receiving an oral tradition that connects in an unbroken chain of teacher-to-student transmission back to the Prophet himself. The chains of transmission for the canonical qira'at (recitation modes) are fully documented in the traditional sciences of recitation.

Al-Kairanawi contrasts this with the manuscript tradition of the New Testament, for which the oldest complete manuscripts date to the fourth century, more than three hundred years after the events they describe, and for which scholars had already identified thousands of variants across the surviving manuscript tradition. He cites the scholarly finding that no two manuscripts of the New Testament are completely identical, reflecting the inevitable variations that arise in handwritten copying over many generations. He notes the significant textual debates about specific passages: the ending of Mark, the story of the woman caught in adultery in John, the comma Johanneum in the first letter of John that explicitly supports the Trinity and appears in the Latin Vulgate but not in the oldest Greek manuscripts. These are not peripheral matters; they involve passages that have been doctrinally significant in Christian theology.

The significance of this comparison for al-Kairanawi's overall argument is substantial. Pfander and other missionaries had claimed that the clear and reliable text of the Christian scriptures provided a superior basis for religious certainty compared to Islamic sources. Al-Kairanawi demonstrates that this confidence in Biblical textual reliability is not warranted by the actual evidence of the manuscript tradition. If textual reliability is a measure of religious authority, then the Quran, with its extraordinary oral transmission tradition and its complete absence of the manuscript variation that characterizes the Biblical texts, stands on far higher ground than the Bible. This argument effectively neutralizes one of the major rhetorical advantages that Christian missionaries had claimed, and it does so by engaging the Christian tradition on its own terms, using the evidence that Christian scholars themselves had assembled.
content_ar: Among the most compelling sections of Izhar al-Haqq is al-Kairanawi's extended comparison between the transmission of القرآن الكريم and the transmission of the Biblical texts. This comparison draws on the same contrast that ابن تيمية had sketched in al-Jawab al-Sahih but develops it with much greater detail and with explicit reference to the evidence that nineteenth-century European Biblical scholarship had assembled about the manuscript tradition of the Bible. Al-Kairanawi's insight was that the same tools of textual criticism that missionaries used to claim the superiority of Christian revelation over earlier traditions could be turned around to demonstrate القرآن الكريم's superior textual reliability compared to the Bible. القرآن الكريم's preservation rests on a dual foundation that is unique among the world's major scriptures: simultaneous written and oral transmission, mutually checking each other. From the earliest years of the revelation, النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم commanded that each new revelation be written down by designated scribes and also committed to memory by الصحابة الكرام رضي الله عنهم. The community that formed around النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم included, from the outset, large numbers of huffaz (those who memorized the entire القرآن الكريم), and the tradition of complete memorization has continued unbroken from that day to the present. A المسلم child memorizing القرآن الكريم today is not merely learning from a book; they are receiving an oral tradition that connects in an unbroken chain of teacher-to-student transmission back to النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم himself. The chains of transmission for the canonical qira'at (recitation modes) are fully documented in the traditional sciences of recitation. Al-Kairanawi contrasts this with the manuscript tradition of the New Testament, for which the oldest complete manuscripts date to the fourth century, more than three hundred years after the events they describe, and for which العلماء had already identified thousands of variants across the surviving manuscript tradition. He cites the scholarly finding that no two manuscripts of the New Testament are completely identical, reflecting the inevitable variations that arise in handwritten copying over many generations. He notes the significant textual debates about specific passages: the ending of Mark, the story of the woman caught in adultery in John, the comma Johanneum in the first letter of John that explicitly supports the Trinity and appears in the Latin Vulgate but not in the oldest Greek manuscripts. These are not peripheral matters; they involve passages that have been doctrinally significant in Christian theology. The significance of this comparison for al-Kairanawi's overall argument is substantial. Pfander and other missionaries had claimed that the clear and reliable text of the Christian scriptures provided a superior basis for religious certainty compared to الإسلامي sources. Al-Kairanawi demonstrates that this confidence in Biblical textual reliability is not warranted by the actual evidence of the manuscript tradition. If textual reliability is a measure of religious authority, then القرآن الكريم, with its extraordinary oral transmission tradition and its complete absence of the manuscript variation that characterizes the Biblical texts, stands on far higher ground than the Bible. This argument effectively neutralizes one of the major rhetorical advantages that Christian missionaries had claimed, and it does so by engaging the Christian tradition on its own terms, using the evidence that Christian العلماء themselves had assembled.
